93WR-TBF: Born around 1627 in a Devon rectory to a minister's family, Elizabeth Hull crossed the Atlantic as a child in 1635 and grew up on the rugged Maine and New Hampshire frontier. She married the Reverend John Heard and raised an extraordinary family of fifteen children. Remembered as a grave and pious woman, she famously survived the 1689 Indian massacre at Dover when warriors, recognizing her past kindness, spared her life.
9MR5-X64: A Massachusetts man born in Lynn in 1637, Edmund Bridges worked as a blacksmith in the bustling colonial settlements of Essex County. He spent his life in and around Salem, where he died in 1682. His estate passed through probate that November, and no headstone was ever placed to mark his resting place.
G3HG-LH6: Born in 1641 in Charles City, Virginia, Robert Lucy rose to the rank of captain and lived as a man of standing in the early Tidewater colony. Court records show him entangled in a 1677 lawsuit over a horse taken for militia use, and in 1678 he was entrusted with the guardianship of orphaned children. Married three times, he fathered eight children before his death.
GJLN-4K5: John Garnett III spent his life in colonial Virginia, settling first in Kingston Parish, Gloucester County, before moving his family to St. Anne's Parish in Essex County around 1692. There he witnessed deeds and held 150 acres of land. He took charge of his late brother Anthony's estate in 1707, and when he died in 1713 his own brother Thomas became trustee for his estate and two young children.
KCSK-3DH: Born in 1881 in the Louisiana community of Knight, named for his own family, John Augustus Knight became a doctor and spent his life serving the small towns of Calcasieu and Beauregard parishes. He registered for the World War I draft and was practicing in Merryville by 1930. He died in DeRidder in 1958, a hometown physician through and through.
KNH8-47K: Ruth Hadley entered the world in 1711 or 1712 in County Westmeath, Ireland, into a Quaker family that soon emigrated to colonial America. She married in Chester County, Pennsylvania, in 1731, and in 1751 the family pushed south to the Carolina frontier, settling near Spring Monthly Meeting in Orange County, North Carolina, where she lived out her days until 1785.
L17H-YKV: Gideon Hogg was born in Hanover, Virginia, in 1722 and later made his home in Caswell County, North Carolina. When the Revolution came, he stood with the cause of independence, recognized today as a documented Patriot of the American Revolution. He died in 1793, having lived long enough to see the new nation take root.
L2SD-DZG: Born in Philadelphia in 1692 to a surveying family of part-Swedish descent, Euclydus Longshore broke from his kin's profession and took up the cobbler's craft, becoming a cordwainer in Bucks County, Pennsylvania. Family memory paints him as a quiet, humble, and honest man, content without ambition for distinction. He settled in Middletown Township and died there in 1764, his will probated that October.
L4T4-7VR: George Little was born in Scotland around 1735, his exact birthplace lost to time, and emigrated to the New World as a young man. He bore arms in military service during the Revolutionary era and by 1790 had settled in Union, South Carolina, before joining the wave of pioneers who poured into Kentucky as the frontier opened. He died in Daviess County, Kentucky, in 1815, remembered as one of the state's early settlers.
L69G-WSL: Born in 1706 into a Quaker family in County Carlow, Ireland, Thomas Lindley emigrated to Pennsylvania as a boy and later carried his family south to the North Carolina frontier, amassing nearly a thousand acres along Cane Creek. In 1755 he built Lindley's Mill, the valley's first gristmill, and helped found a Friends meeting for worship. His mill became the site of a 1781 Revolutionary battle, and he died the day after the fighting.
L87P-XVJ: David Hext was born in Charleston, South Carolina, in 1686, the son of British parents, and lived his whole life in that colonial port city. Records note his military service, and he died in Charleston in 1754, a lifelong son of the Carolina lowcountry.
LBJN-71R: John Neville sailed from the Isle of Wight aboard Lord Baltimore's famed ships, the Ark and the Dove, landing in Maryland in March 1634 among the colony's very first settlers. A mariner by trade, he acquired land at Poplar Hill in Talbot County and married twice. He helped shape the earliest years of colonial Maryland before his death at Port Tobacco in 1664.
LCCH-GKV: Born in 1656 in Dutch New Amsterdam, Casparus Augustine Herman came of age as colonial worlds shifted around him. He became a planter and merchant with commercial ties reaching into New York, and carried the rank of Colonel. He lived on the Maryland estate of Bohemia Manor in Cecil County, where he was buried after his death in 1711.
LD58-CQM: Thomas Jackson was born in Virginia in 1739 and made his home in Prince Edward County. When the colonies rose against Britain, he took up the Patriot cause, earning recognition for his Revolutionary War service. He died in Virginia in 1792, his contribution to American independence preserved in the records of the new nation he helped to win.
LHBP-VB5: Born in 1628 in Elizabeth City, Virginia, Lemuel Mason became one of the most prominent figures of colonial Lower Norfolk County. He served as a justice from 1650 until his death, rose to colonel of the militia, and was repeatedly elected a Burgess across more than three decades. During the Indian conflicts of 1675 and 1676 he was authorized to impress men into service, a leader of his county to the end in 1702.
LHZD-3WY: George Chisolm Sr. was born in Charleston, South Carolina, in 1772 and lived his entire life in that coastal city through the early decades of the young republic. Court records trace his affairs across more than three decades, from 1800 to the year of his death in 1835.
LKKN-QFN: William Hamick was born around 1623 in Ermington, Devon, England, and crossed the Atlantic in 1656, his passage among six persons recorded on a Northampton County land patent. He built his life in colonial Virginia, settling in North Farnham Parish of Richmond County, where he died around 1700, a Devon emigrant who became a Virginia planter.
LRGX-K2H: Born in London in 1632, Elizabeth Underwood came to Virginia and lived a turbulent life on the Old Rappahannock frontier. Records tell of four marriages and a divorce in 1654, a striking story for a colonial woman. She wrote her will in 1672, naming her son, and it was entered for probate after her death the following year.
LT81-L1F: Michel Boudrot left La Rochelle, France, and settled at Port-Royal in Acadia by 1639, becoming the founding ancestor of the great Acadian Boudreau family. A farmer who raised eleven children with his wife Michelle Aucoin, he rose to serve as judge and lieutenant general of Port-Royal. In 1693 he was even sent on a discreet intelligence mission to Boston, which he carried out with success before his death in 1688.
LVNC-WSJ: Born in France around 1601, Jean Therault was among the earliest pioneers of Acadia, arriving at Port-Royal with his wife Perrine Rau in the 1630s. A laboureur, or plowman, he raised seven children on his modest farm. His son Pierre would go on to help found the famous Acadian settlement of Grand-Pré, extending the family's roots deep into Acadian history.
LX9M-4NN: Robert Longshore came from Wigan, Lancashire, England, settling in William Penn's new colony before 1681. A skilled surveyor, he served as Deputy Surveyor and helped lay out the original plan of Philadelphia in 1682. A gentleman of means, he purchased five hundred acres in Bristol township before his death in Philadelphia in 1694.
LYDG-7DX: Sir Thomas Montgomery was born in County Down, Ireland, around 1650 and bore the title of knight. A sailor by occupation, he eventually made his way across the Atlantic and was associated with both Indiana and Kentucky, ending his days in Lexington, Fayette County, Kentucky.
LZ6B-F79: Diana Skipwith was baptized in 1621 at Prestwold in Leicestershire, England, a lady of gentle birth. She crossed the Atlantic to Virginia around 1655, joining the colonial gentry of the Tidewater. She lived out her years in Lancaster County, Virginia, where she died in 1695.
LZG3-3BW: Born in 1654 in Isle of Wight County, Virginia, William Basse was of Nansemond Native American descent through his mother. In 1727 he was forced to formally prove his Native ancestry rather than African, a desperate necessity in an era when any presumed African blood could cost a family its land, its rights, and its very freedom. His 1740 will, made when he was sick and weak, carefully divided his land and few possessions among his children.
LZSS-S4L: Sarah Towne was born in Salem, Massachusetts, in 1637 into a family that would be scarred by tragedy. During the Salem witch hysteria of 1692 she was accused of witchcraft, the same accusation that sent her own sister to the gallows. Sarah escaped execution, later remarried, and lived on until 1703, dying in Framingham far from the village that had turned against her.
MF72-GP7: Henry Barnes Jr. was born in London, England, in 1653 and immigrated that same year to colonial Maryland. His life was brief, ending in Charles County, Maryland, in 1676 when he was only about twenty-three years old.
MW2B-7R4: Abraham Garrett was born in Laurens, South Carolina, in 1796 and served his country as a soldier in the War of 1812. He later settled in Georgia, working as a brickmason and raising his family in Jackson County. He lived a long life, dying in Gwinnett County in 1882 at the age of eighty-six.
